Goco dynamics and garrison project related/
Claude finished the response

You said: What are got hub and rail way said how can I use them to builds a website for goco and the garrison?

What are got hub and rail way said how can I use them to builds a website for goco and the garrison?

Mar 19

Claude responded: Good question.

Searched the web

Good question. Here's the straight breakdown — no tech jargon.

GitHub is where your website's code lives. Think of it as a filing cabinet in the cloud. Every change you make gets saved and tracked. If something breaks, you can roll back. If someone helps you build it, they can access the same files. It's free and it's where developers store and manage code.

Railway is what takes that code and puts it on the internet. It's a full-stack cloud platform for deploying web apps with automatic scaling and security. You connect your GitHub account to Railway, and every time you update your code, Railway automatically pushes those changes live. It's beginner-friendly, offers a free tier, gives you automatic HTTPS, and integrates directly with GitHub for continuous deployment. RailwayCraftedTemplate

The workflow is simple: GitHub = your code. Railway = your live website.

Here's how you'd use them for GoCo and the Garrison Project:

Two separate sites — one for each. GoCo is the professional LLC, Garrison is the nonprofit. Different audiences, different messaging, different calls to action. Keep them separate.

Step 1 — Build your sites. You can use something like Claude or a developer to generate the actual HTML/CSS code. You don't need to write it yourself.

Step 2 — Push to GitHub. Create two repositories. One called goco-dynamics, one called garrison-project. Your code goes in there.

Step 3 — Connect to Railway. Sign up at Railway using your GitHub account, click New Project, choose "Deploy from GitHub repo," and select your repo. Railway builds it automatically and gives you a public URL. CraftedTemplate

Step 4 — Add your domain. Go to Settings → Domains in Railway and add your custom domain — like gacocsynamics.com or garrisonproject.org — then update your DNS records as instructed. CraftedTemplate

What you actually need to do first:

Before any of this matters — you need the content. What's on each site. GoCo needs to position you as the professional arm, the seminar and training side. Garrison needs to lead with the mission, the outcomes, and a way for people to get involved or donate.

You want help building out either of those sites? That's the real starting point. The tech is just how it gets published.

You said: Yes let’s start with goco.

Yes let’s start with goco.

Mar 19

Claude responded: Let me ask a few quick questions so we build the right thing from jump.

Let me ask a few quick questions so we build the right thing from jump.

Answer those three and we'll start building.

You said: Q: What's the primary goal of the GoCo site right now?

Q: What's the primary goal of the GoCo site right now? A: Establish credibility and legitimacy

Q: Who's the main audience landing on this site? A: Social work agencies / organizations, Nonprofits and schools, Individual social workers, and Potential funders or partners

Q: What pages do you want on the site? A: Home / About, Services (seminars, training), The Garrison Project connection, and Contact / Book Angelo

Mar 19

Claude responded: Good.

Reading frontend design skill before building

Good. Four pages, one goal — make anyone who lands on this site take Angelo seriously before they even scroll. Let me build it.

Created a file, read a file

There it is. Dark, sharp, built to establish credibility the second someone lands on it. Here's what's in it:

Home — leads with the core value proposition. Survivability training. Proactive not reactive. Three stat callouts for the credentials that matter.

About — Angelo's background laid out clean. MSW, BJJ, Evolve, Garrison. No fluff.

Services — three cards. Situational Awareness, Verbal De-Escalation, Physical Readiness. Each with format and audience tags so agencies know exactly what they're booking.

Garrison Project — positioned as the mission. GoCo is the engine, Garrison is the purpose. Outcome-focused — grades, life skills, not feelings.

Contact / Book — form goes straight to Angelo. No middleman framing.

A few things you'll want to swap in before this goes live: your actual email address on the contact form (right now it just confirms on-screen), your LinkedIn or any social handles for the footer, and a real phone number or email in the contact details section if you want that visible.

Want to adjust anything — copy, colors, layout, what's emphasized?

Goco dynamics
Code · HTML 

You said: Ok can I make those changes when I put the code to railway?

Ok can I make those changes when I put the code to railway?

Mar 19

Claude responded: Not directly on Railway — Railway is just the hosting.

Not directly on Railway — Railway is just the hosting. It publishes what you give it.

Here's the actual workflow:

Where you make changes: In the HTML file itself. You have three options depending on how hands-on you want to get:

  1. Easiest — Tell me what to change right here and I update the file for you. Swap in your email, phone, any edits to the copy. You download the final version and push that to Railway.
  2. Middle ground — Open the file in a basic text editor (even Notepad works) and find-and-replace things like your email address directly. Simple stuff only.
  3. Developer route — Use VS Code (free), which gives you a proper code editor with color coding so it's easier to navigate. This is worth learning if you're going to maintain both sites long term.

For right now, the fastest move is option 1. Tell me what needs to go in:

  • Your email address for the contact form
  • Your phone number (if you want it visible)
  • Any copy that's off

We get the file clean, THEN you push it to GitHub and deploy on Railway. That's the order.

What needs to change?

You said: Email: angelocoates@yahoo.

Email: angelocoates@yahoo.com for now. I don’t want the number on right now.

Mar 19

Claude responded: Done.

Edited 3 files, read a file

Done. Two changes made:

  1. Email visible in the contact details section — angelocoates@yahoo.com
  2. Form is wired up — when someone submits it, it opens their email client with everything pre-filled (their name, org, what they're inquiring about, their message) addressed to you. Clean, no backend needed.

This file is ready to push to Railway. Want me to walk you through the GitHub and Railway setup step by step?

Goco dynamics
Code · HTML 

You said: Yes

Yes

Mar 19

Claude responded: Here's the exact step by step.

Here's the exact step by step. Do these in order.

STEP 1 — Create a GitHub account

Go to github.com and sign up. Free account is all you need. Use your yahoo email to keep it simple.

STEP 2 — Create a new repository

Once you're in:

  • Click the + button in the top right
  • Click New repository
  • Name it: goco-dynamics
  • Set it to Public
  • Check the box that says Add a README file
  • Click Create repository

STEP 3 — Upload your file

Inside your new repository:

  • Click Add file → Upload files
  • Drag and drop the goco-dynamics.html file you downloaded
  • Scroll down and click Commit changes

One important thing — rename the file before you upload it. Change goco-dynamics.html to index.html. Railway looks for a file called index.html to know what to display as your homepage.

STEP 4 — Create a Railway account

Go to railway.com:

  • Click Login
  • Choose Login with GitHub
  • Authorize it — this connects your GitHub directly

STEP 5 — Deploy your site

Once you're inside Railway:

  • Click New Project
  • Click Deploy from GitHub repo
  • Select goco-dynamics
  • Railway builds it automatically — takes about 60 seconds

STEP 6 — Get your live URL

After it deploys:

  • Click into your project
  • Click Settings → Networking → Generate Domain
  • Railway gives you a URL like goco-dynamics.up.railway.app

That's your live site.

STEP 7 — Custom domain (when you're ready)

When you buy gocodynamics.com or whatever domain you want, come back to that same Settings → Networking section and add it there. Railway walks you through the DNS setup.

Start with Step 1 and go through it. Come back here when you get stuck or when something doesn't look right and we'll sort it out.

You said: Can I paste the code somewhere?

Can I paste the code somewhere? I can download the file

Mar 19

Claude responded: Yes.

Yes. Two options:

Option 1 — Paste directly in GitHub (easiest)

Once your repository is created in Step 2:

  • Click Add file → Create new file
  • Name the file index.html
  • Paste the entire code into the big text box below the filename
  • Scroll down and click Commit changes

Done. No downloading, no uploading.

Option 2 — Download and upload

If you already downloaded the file:

  • Rename it from goco-dynamics.html to index.html on your computer first
  • Then go back to Step 3 and upload it

Option 1 is faster. You already have the code — just copy it from the file preview, go to GitHub, create the file, paste it in.

Want me to pull the full code back up so it's easy to copy?

You said: What happened?

What happened?

Mar 19

Claude responded: Good — you got further than most people do on the first try.

Good — you got further than most people do on the first try. You're in Railway, it connected to GitHub, it tried to build. That's all right.

The error is "Error creating build plan with Railpack." Railway is trying to figure out what kind of app you have and getting confused. It's looking for a Node.js app or Python app — something with a build process. All you have is a plain HTML file. It doesn't need to be built, it just needs to be served.

The fix — add one config file to your GitHub repo.

Go back to your GitHub repository and do this:

  • Click Add file → Create new file
  • Name it exactly: railway.json
  • Paste this in:
json
{
  "$schema": "https://railway.com/railway.schema.json",
  "build": {
    "builder": "NIXPACKS"
  },
  "deploy": {
    "startCommand": "npx serve . -p $PORT"
  }
}
  • Click Commit changes

That tells Railway — stop trying to build anything, just serve the HTML file directly. Railway will automatically retry the deployment when you commit that file.

Come back and show me what it says after that.

Mar 19

Claude responded: Same error.

Same error. The railway.json file isn't overriding it the way I expected. Let's fix this a different way — add one more file that tells Railway exactly how to serve the site.

Go back to your GitHub repo and create another new file:

  • Click Add file → Create new file
  • Name it exactly: package.json
  • Paste this in:
json
{
  "name": "goco-dynamics",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "npx serve . -p $PORT"
  }
}
  • Click Commit changes

That gives Railway the build signal it's looking for. It'll see a package.json, recognize it as a Node project, and use the start command to serve your HTML file directly.

Should trigger a new deploy automatically. Show me what it says after.

You said: Region: us-west1]

Region: us-west1]

╭─────────────────╮ │ Railpack 0.20.0 │ ╰─────────────────╯

⚠ Script start.sh not found ✖ Railpack could not determine how to build the app.

The following languages are supported: Php Golang Java Rust Ruby Elixir Python Deno Dotnet Node Gleam Cpp Staticfile Shell

The app contents that Railpack analyzed contains:

./ ├── Goco code.pdf ├── Package.json ├── README.md └── Railway.json

Check out the docs for more information: https://railpack.com

Mar 19

Claude responded: Found the problem.

Found the problem. Two things:

  1. Your files are named Package.json and Railway.json with capital letters. They need to be lowercase — package.json and railway.json. GitHub on mobile sometimes auto-capitalizes.
  2. Railway doesn't see your index.html at all — it's not showing in that file list. That means it either wasn't uploaded or got saved with a different name.

Fix all of it at once. Go to your GitHub repo and:

First — delete or rename Package.json. Open it, click the pencil icon to edit, change the name at the top from Package.json to package.json, then commit.

Do the same for Railway.json → railway.json.

Then check if index.html is in the repo. If you don't see it in the file list, you need to add it — click Add file → Create new file, name it index.html, paste the site code in, and commit.

Once all three files are in there with the right names — index.html, package.json, railway.json — Railway should build clean.

What files do you see when you look at your GitHub repo right now?

Mar 19

Claude responded: Still the same error and I can see why — it says Create Index.

Still the same error and I can see why — it says Create Index.html with a capital I. Same capitalization problem. Railway needs all lowercase.

The issue is GitHub on mobile is auto-capitalizing your filenames. You need to fix all three files to be lowercase. Here's the cleanest way to do it from your phone:

For each file — Index.html, Package.json, Railway.json:

  1. Open the file in GitHub
  2. Tap the pencil icon to edit
  3. At the very top you'll see the filename in a text box — tap it and manually change the capital letter to lowercase
  4. Scroll down and tap Commit changes

Do that for all three files so your repo shows:

  • index.html
  • package.json
  • railway.json

All lowercase, no exceptions.

Railway will auto-trigger a new build after each commit. Once all three are lowercase it should go through. Show me what it says after you fix them.

Mar 19

Claude responded: That's it.

That's it. ACTIVE. Deployment successful. All four checkmarks green.

GoCo Dynamics is live.

Now get your URL. In Railway:

  • Tap Settings at the top
  • Scroll to Networking
  • Tap Generate Domain

It'll give you a link like goco-dynamics.up.railway.app. That's your live site. Open it in a browser and you'll see the full thing.

Send me the URL when you have it.

Claude responded: It's asking what port your app is running on.

It's asking what port your app is running on.

Change 8080 to 3000 — that's the port the serve package uses by default — then tap Generate Domain.

Mar 19

Claude responded: You're live.

You're live. That's your URL right there — tap the copy icon next to goco-dynamics-production.u... to get the full link, then open it in your browser.

GoCo Dynamics is on the internet. From zero to deployed in one session.

When you're ready to get a real domain — gocodynamics.com or whatever you land on — you buy it through GoDaddy or Namecheap, then come back to that same Networking screen and tap Custom Domain. Railway walks you through pointing it to your site.

Next step after that is building the Garrison Project site. Same process, separate repo. Want to start on that?

Mar 19

Claude responded: The site deployed but the app isn't responding on port 3000.

The site deployed but the app isn't responding on port 3000. The serve package is probably running on a different port. Easy fix.

Go to your GitHub repo and open package.json. Edit it and replace everything in there with this:

json
{
  "name": "goco-dynamics",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "npx serve . --listen $PORT"
  }
}

The difference is --listen $PORT instead of -p $PORT. This tells serve to use whatever port Railway assigns dynamically instead of assuming 3000.

Commit the change. Railway will redeploy automatically. Then try the URL again.

You said: I committed changes and it didn’t fix it

I committed changes and it didn’t fix it

Mar 19

Claude responded: Let's check the deploy logs to see exactly what's happening.

Let's check the deploy logs to see exactly what's happening. In Railway:

  • Tap into your project
  • Tap the active deployment
  • Look for a Logs tab or scroll down to see the log output

Screenshot that and send it here. That'll tell us exactly what the app is doing when it tries to start.

You said: Starting Container

Starting Container cleaning storage unit automatic HTTPS is completely disabled for server finished cleaning storage units started background certificate maintenance HTTP/2 skipped because it requires TLS HTTP/3 skipped because it requires TLS server running serving initial configuration maxprocs: Updating GOMAXPROCS=2: determined from CPU quota GOMEMLIMIT is updated using config from file adapted config to JSON admin endpoint disabled

Mar 19

Claude responded: The server is running.

The server is running. The issue is it's not listening on the right port.

Go to Railway → your project → Settings → Networking and look at what port is listed. Then delete that domain and regenerate it — but this time when it asks for the port, leave it blank or try 8080 instead of 3000.

Actually — easier fix. Go back to your GitHub repo and update package.json to this:

json
{
  "name": "goco-dynamics",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "npx serve . -l 8080"
  }
}

Then in Railway Settings → Networking, delete the current domain, regenerate it, and enter 8080 as the port this time.

Commit that, let it redeploy, then try the URL again.

Website looks like this

Two things to fix — the font loading and the stat strip overlapping the text on mobile. I'll update the file to embed the font differently and tighten the mobile layout.

You said: How do I redeploy it after commiting

How do I redeploy it after commiting

Mar 19

Claude responded: Railway redeploys automatically every time you commit to GitHub.

Railway redeploys automatically every time you commit to GitHub. You don't have to do anything.

Just commit the updated index.html to your GitHub repo and Railway picks it up within about 60 seconds. Watch the Deployments tab in Railway — you'll see a new build start on its own.

That's one of the best things about this setup. GitHub and Railway are connected. Every commit = automatic redeploy. You never have to touch Railway again unless you're changing settings.

That's just the log from the previous deployment — it's showing you old history.

Mar 19

Claude responded: The stat strip is still overlapping.

The stat strip is still overlapping. The new code didn't make it in — Railway is probably still serving the old version.

The fonts are a separate issue — your phone's Safari is blocking Google Fonts as a privacy measure. That's actually common on iPhone. It'll load fine on desktop and on most other browsers.

Let's fix the overlap. That stat strip needs to be fully removed from the mobile view. Go to your GitHub repo, open index.html, tap the pencil, and find this section — it's the three stat blocks on the right side of the hero. Search for hero-stat-strip and delete everything from <div class="hero-stat-strip"> all the way to the closing </div> that ends that block.

Or easier — just tell me and I'll send you a clean updated file with that entire block removed from the HTML entirely, not just hidden with CSS. That's the guaranteed fix. CSS hiding isn't working on your browser — physically removing the HTML is cleaner anyway.

Which do you want to do?

Send the new one and I’ll paste it over the whole other code

Claude responded: There it is.

Viewed 3 files, edited a file, read a file

There it is. Copy all the code from that file, go to GitHub, open index.html, tap the pencil, select all, paste, commit. Railway redeploys in about a minute and the overlap is gone.

The fonts will look better on desktop — Safari on iPhone blocks external font requests by default with its privacy settings. Anyone visiting on a laptop or non-Apple browser will see the full designed version.

<div class="hero-stat-strip">

You said: <!

<!DOCTYPE html>

<html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> <title>GoCo Dynamics | Survivability Training for Helping Professionals</title> <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com"> <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin> <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Bebas+Neue&family=DM+Sans:wght@300;400;500;700&family=DM+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap" rel="stylesheet"> <style> @import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Bebas+Neue&family=DM+Sans:wght@300;400;500;700&family=DM+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap'); </style> <style> :root { --black: #0a0a0a; --off-black: #111111; --charcoal: #1c1c1c; --steel: #2e2e2e; --mid: #555; --muted: #888; --light: #c8c8c8; --white: #f5f3ef; --accent: #c8410a; --accent-light: #e05020; --rule: rgba(255,255,255,0.08); }

*, *::before, *::after { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }

html { scroll-behavior: smooth; }

body { background: var(–black); color: var(–white); font-family: ‘DM Sans’, sans-serif; font-weight: 300; line-height: 1.65; overflow-x: hidden; }

/* ── NAV ── */ nav { position: fixed; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0; z-index: 100; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: space-between; padding: 1.25rem 4rem; border-bottom: 1px solid var(–rule); background: rgba(10,10,10,0.92); backdrop-filter: blur(12px); }

.nav-logo { font-family: ‘Bebas Neue’, sans-serif; font-size: 1.6rem; letter-spacing: 0.12em; color: var(–white); text-decoration: none; }

.nav-logo span { color: var(–accent); }

.nav-links { display: flex; gap: 2.5rem; list-style: none; }

.nav-links a { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.72rem; letter-spacing: 0.18em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–light); text-decoration: none; transition: color 0.2s; }

.nav-links a:hover { color: var(–accent); }

.nav-cta { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.72rem; letter-spacing: 0.15em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–white); background: var(–accent); border: none; padding: 0.65rem 1.5rem; cursor: pointer; text-decoration: none; transition: background 0.2s; }

.nav-cta:hover { background: var(–accent-light); }

/* ── HERO ── */ #home { min-height: 100vh; display: flex; align-items: flex-end; padding: 0 4rem 6rem; position: relative; overflow: hidden; }

.hero-bg { position: absolute; inset: 0; background: radial-gradient(ellipse 60% 50% at 70% 40%, rgba(200,65,10,0.12) 0%, transparent 70%), radial-gradient(ellipse 40% 60% at 20% 80%, rgba(200,65,10,0.06) 0%, transparent 60%), var(–off-black); }

.hero-grid { position: absolute; inset: 0; background-image: linear-gradient(var(–rule) 1px, transparent 1px), linear-gradient(90deg, var(–rule) 1px, transparent 1px); background-size: 60px 60px; mask-image: radial-gradient(ellipse 80% 80% at 50% 50%, black 30%, transparent 100%); }

.hero-content { position: relative; z-index: 2; max-width: 900px; }

.hero-eyebrow { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.72rem; letter-spacing: 0.25em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–accent); margin-bottom: 1.5rem; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 1rem; }

.hero-eyebrow::before { content: ‘’; display: block; width: 40px; height: 1px; background: var(–accent); }

.hero-headline { font-family: ‘Bebas Neue’, sans-serif; font-size: clamp(4rem, 9vw, 9rem); line-height: 0.92; letter-spacing: 0.02em; color: var(–white); margin-bottom: 2rem; }

.hero-headline .accent-line { color: var(–accent); }

.hero-sub { font-size: 1.15rem; font-weight: 300; color: var(–light); max-width: 540px; margin-bottom: 3rem; line-height: 1.7; }

.hero-actions { display: flex; gap: 1.25rem; align-items: center; }

.btn-primary { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.75rem; letter-spacing: 0.18em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–white); background: var(–accent); border: none; padding: 1rem 2.25rem; cursor: pointer; text-decoration: none; transition: background 0.2s; display: inline-block; }

.btn-primary:hover { background: var(–accent-light); }

.btn-ghost { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.75rem; letter-spacing: 0.18em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–light); background: transparent; border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.2); padding: 1rem 2.25rem; cursor: pointer; text-decoration: none; transition: border-color 0.2s, color 0.2s; display: inline-block; }

.btn-ghost:hover { border-color: var(–accent); color: var(–accent); }

.hero-stat-strip { position: absolute; right: 4rem; bottom: 6rem; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 2rem; z-index: 2; }

.hero-stat { text-align: right; }

.hero-stat-number { font-family: ‘Bebas Neue’, sans-serif; font-size: 2.8rem; color: var(–accent); line-height: 1; }

.hero-stat-label { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.62rem; letter-spacing: 0.2em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–muted); display: block; }

/* ── SECTION BASE ── */ section { padding: 7rem 4rem; }

.section-label { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.7rem; letter-spacing: 0.25em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–accent); margin-bottom: 1rem; display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 1rem; }

.section-label::before { content: ‘’; display: block; width: 32px; height: 1px; background: var(–accent); }

.section-title { font-family: ‘Bebas Neue’, sans-serif; font-size: clamp(3rem, 5vw, 5rem); line-height: 0.95; letter-spacing: 0.02em; color: var(–white); margin-bottom: 1.5rem; }

.divider { border: none; border-top: 1px solid var(–rule); margin: 0; }

/* ── ABOUT ── */ #about { background: var(–off-black); }

.about-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 5rem; align-items: start; margin-top: 3rem; }

.about-left p { font-size: 1.05rem; color: var(–light); margin-bottom: 1.5rem; line-height: 1.75; }

.about-left p strong { color: var(–white); font-weight: 500; }

.about-right { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 0; }

.credential-item { display: flex; gap: 1.5rem; align-items: flex-start; padding: 1.75rem 0; border-bottom: 1px solid var(–rule); }

.credential-item:first-child { border-top: 1px solid var(–rule); }

.credential-num { font-family: ‘Bebas Neue’, sans-serif; font-size: 1.6rem; color: var(–accent); line-height: 1; flex-shrink: 0; width: 2.5rem; padding-top: 0.1rem; }

.credential-text strong { display: block; font-weight: 500; font-size: 0.95rem; color: var(–white); margin-bottom: 0.25rem; }

.credential-text span { font-size: 0.85rem; color: var(–muted); line-height: 1.5; }

/* ── SERVICES ── */ #services { background: var(–black); }

.services-intro { max-width: 600px; font-size: 1.05rem; color: var(–light); line-height: 1.75; margin-bottom: 4rem; }

.services-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); gap: 0; border: 1px solid var(–rule); }

.service-card { padding: 2.75rem 2.25rem; border-right: 1px solid var(–rule); position: relative; transition: background 0.25s; }

.service-card:last-child { border-right: none; }

.service-card:hover { background: rgba(200,65,10,0.05); }

.service-card::before { content: ‘’; position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0; height: 2px; background: var(–accent); transform: scaleX(0); transform-origin: left; transition: transform 0.3s; }

.service-card:hover::before { transform: scaleX(1); }

.service-icon { font-family: ‘Bebas Neue’, sans-serif; font-size: 3rem; color: var(–accent); line-height: 1; margin-bottom: 1.25rem; opacity: 0.7; }

.service-name { font-family: ‘Bebas Neue’, sans-serif; font-size: 1.6rem; letter-spacing: 0.04em; color: var(–white); margin-bottom: 0.75rem; }

.service-desc { font-size: 0.9rem; color: var(–muted); line-height: 1.7; margin-bottom: 1.5rem; }

.service-tags { display: flex; flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 0.4rem; }

.tag { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.62rem; letter-spacing: 0.12em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–muted); border: 1px solid var(–rule); padding: 0.3rem 0.65rem; }

/* ── GARRISON ── */ #garrison { background: var(–charcoal); position: relative; overflow: hidden; }

.garrison-accent-bg { position: absolute; top: -100px; right: -100px; width: 500px; height: 500px; border-radius: 50%; background: radial-gradient(circle, rgba(200,65,10,0.08) 0%, transparent 70%); pointer-events: none; }

.garrison-layout { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 6rem; align-items: center; position: relative; z-index: 1; }

.garrison-left p { font-size: 1.05rem; color: var(–light); line-height: 1.75; margin-bottom: 1.5rem; }

.garrison-left p strong { color: var(–white); font-weight: 500; }

.garrison-pill { display: inline-block; font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.68rem; letter-spacing: 0.18em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–accent); border: 1px solid var(–accent); padding: 0.4rem 1rem; margin-bottom: 1.5rem; }

.garrison-right { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 0; }

.outcome-row { display: flex; align-items: flex-start; gap: 1.5rem; padding: 1.5rem 0; border-bottom: 1px solid var(–rule); }

.outcome-row:first-child { border-top: 1px solid var(–rule); }

.outcome-icon { width: 8px; height: 8px; background: var(–accent); flex-shrink: 0; margin-top: 0.55rem; }

.outcome-text strong { display: block; font-weight: 500; font-size: 0.95rem; color: var(–white); margin-bottom: 0.2rem; }

.outcome-text span { font-size: 0.85rem; color: var(–muted); line-height: 1.5; }

/* ── CONTACT ── */ #contact { background: var(–off-black); }

.contact-layout { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 6rem; margin-top: 3rem; }

.contact-left p { font-size: 1.05rem; color: var(–light); line-height: 1.75; margin-bottom: 2rem; }

.contact-detail { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 1.25rem; margin-bottom: 3rem; }

.contact-detail-item { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 0.2rem; }

.contact-detail-label { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.65rem; letter-spacing: 0.2em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–accent); }

.contact-detail-value { font-size: 0.95rem; color: var(–light); }

.contact-form { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 1.25rem; }

.form-row { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 1rem; }

.form-group { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 0.4rem; }

.form-group label { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.65rem; letter-spacing: 0.18em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–muted); }

.form-group input, .form-group select, .form-group textarea { background: var(–charcoal); border: 1px solid var(–rule); color: var(–white); font-family: ‘DM Sans’, sans-serif; font-size: 0.95rem; padding: 0.85rem 1rem; outline: none; transition: border-color 0.2s; appearance: none; -webkit-appearance: none; }

.form-group input:focus, .form-group select:focus, .form-group textarea:focus { border-color: var(–accent); }

.form-group textarea { resize: vertical; min-height: 130px; }

.form-group input::placeholder, .form-group textarea::placeholder { color: var(–mid); }

.form-submit { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.75rem; letter-spacing: 0.18em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–white); background: var(–accent); border: none; padding: 1.1rem 2.5rem; cursor: pointer; align-self: flex-start; transition: background 0.2s; }

.form-submit:hover { background: var(–accent-light); }

/* ── FOOTER ── */ footer { background: var(–black); border-top: 1px solid var(–rule); padding: 2.5rem 4rem; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: space-between; }

.footer-logo { font-family: ‘Bebas Neue’, sans-serif; font-size: 1.4rem; letter-spacing: 0.12em; color: var(–white); }

.footer-logo span { color: var(–accent); }

.footer-copy { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.62rem; letter-spacing: 0.12em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–mid); }

.footer-links { display: flex; gap: 2rem; list-style: none; }

.footer-links a { font-family: ‘DM Mono’, monospace; font-size: 0.62rem; letter-spacing: 0.15em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(–muted); text-decoration: none; transition: color 0.2s; }

.footer-links a:hover { color: var(–accent); }

/* ── ANIMATIONS ── */ @keyframes fadeUp { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateY(30px); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateY(0); } }

.hero-eyebrow { animation: fadeUp 0.7s ease both; animation-delay: 0.1s; } .hero-headline { animation: fadeUp 0.7s ease both; animation-delay: 0.25s; } .hero-sub { animation: fadeUp 0.7s ease both; animation-delay: 0.4s; } .hero-actions { animation: fadeUp 0.7s ease both; animation-delay: 0.55s; } .hero-stat-strip { animation: fadeUp 0.7s ease both; animation-delay: 0.7s; }

/* ── RESPONSIVE ── */ @media (max-width: 900px) { nav { padding: 1.25rem 2rem; } .nav-links { display: none; } section { padding: 5rem 2rem; } #home { padding: 8rem 2rem 4rem; min-height: auto; } .about-grid, .garrison-layout, .contact-layout { grid-template-columns: 1fr; gap: 3rem; } .services-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .service-card { border-right: none; border-bottom: 1px solid var(–rule); } .hero-stat-strip { display: none; } footer { flex-direction: column; gap: 1.5rem; text-align: center; } .form-row { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } .hero-headline { font-size: clamp(3rem, 14vw, 5rem); } } </style>

</head> <body>

<!-- NAV -->

<nav> <a href="#home" class="nav-logo">Go<span>Co</span> Dynamics</a> <ul class="nav-links"> <li><a href="#about">About</a></li> <li><a href="#services">Services</a></li> <li><a href="#garrison">Garrison Project</a></li> <li><a href="#contact">Contact</a></li> </ul> <a href="#contact" class="nav-cta">Book a Seminar</a> </nav>

<!-- HERO -->

<section id="home"> <div class="hero-bg"></div> <div class="hero-grid"></div> <div class="hero-content"> <div class="hero-eyebrow">GoCo Dynamics LLC &mdash; San Diego, CA</div> <h1 class="hero-headline"> Built to<br> <span class="accent-line">Survive</span><br> the Field. </h1> <p class="hero-sub"> Survivability training for social workers, case managers, and helping professionals. Situational awareness. Verbal de-escalation. Physical readiness — within the NASW code of ethics. </p> <div class="hero-actions"> <a href="#services" class="btn-primary">See Our Training</a> <a href="#about" class="btn-ghost">About Angelo</a> </div> </div> </section>

<hr class="divider">

<!-- ABOUT -->

<section id="about"> <div class="section-label">About</div> <h2 class="section-title">Proactive.<br>Not Reactive.</h2> <div class="about-grid"> <div class="about-left"> <p> The social work field trains professionals to handle almost everything — <strong>except staying safe when things go sideways.</strong> The NASW code of ethics is solid on principles. It is silent on what to do when a client becomes a threat. </p> <p> GoCo Dynamics fills that gap. Founded by Angelo Coates, MSW candidate and Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu practitioner, GoCo delivers survivability skills built specifically for the helping professions — grounded in the do-no-harm framework and designed for real field situations. </p> <p> This is not self-defense theater. It is <strong>situational awareness, verbal de-escalation, and physical readiness</strong> — in that order, every time. The goal is never to fight. The goal is to get home. </p> <p> GoCo is the professional arm. The mission runs through the Garrison Project, a youth mentorship nonprofit built on the same philosophy: build it before you need it. </p> </div> <div class="about-right"> <div class="credential-item"> <div class="credential-num">01</div> <div class="credential-text"> <strong>MSW Candidate — San Diego State University</strong> <span>Advanced standing. Clinical social work. Focus on youth development and trauma-informed practice.</span> </div> </div> <div class="credential-item"> <div class="credential-num">02</div> <div class="credential-text"> <strong>Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu Practitioner</strong> <span>Competed and trained for years. Draws directly on BJJ principles of leverage, control, and disengagement for field safety application.</span> </div> </div> <div class="credential-item"> <div class="credential-num">03</div> <div class="credential-text"> <strong>Youth Program Experience — Evolve Treatment Center</strong> <span>Direct practice teaching CBT to adolescents. Field-tested curriculum development with at-risk youth populations.</span> </div> </div> <div class="credential-item"> <div class="credential-num">04</div> <div class="credential-text"> <strong>Founder — The Garrison Project</strong> <span>Nonprofit initiative delivering holistic mentorship and jiu-jitsu-based resilience training to fatherless young men.</span> </div> </div> <div class="credential-item"> <div class="credential-num">05</div> <div class="credential-text"> <strong>26+ Years of Physical Discipline</strong> <span>Consistent training and nutrition practice since age 12. Proactive health and physical readiness is not a phase — it is the framework.</span> </div> </div> </div> </div> </section>

<hr class="divider">

<!-- SERVICES -->

<section id="services"> <div class="section-label">Services</div> <h2 class="section-title">What We<br>Deliver.</h2> <p class="services-intro"> Every GoCo training is built around one question: what does a social worker actually need to stay safe? We deliver that — in seminars, workshops, and online refreshers that fit inside your agency's professional development calendar. </p> <div class="services-grid"> <div class="service-card"> <div class="service-icon">01</div> <div class="service-name">Situational Awareness Training</div> <p class="service-desc"> Read the room before it turns. Learn to identify escalation cues, environmental threats, and behavioral signals that precede violence — so you can act before a situation requires physical response. </p> <div class="service-tags"> <span class="tag">Half-day seminar</span> <span class="tag">Full-day workshop</span> <span class="tag">Agency groups</span> </div> </div> <div class="service-card"> <div class="service-icon">02</div> <div class="service-name">Verbal De-Escalation</div> <p class="service-desc"> Words are your first tool. This training builds practical language and communication skills for high-tension client interactions — grounded in real field scenarios, not role-play theater. </p> <div class="service-tags"> <span class="tag">Seminar format</span> <span class="tag">Online refresher</span> <span class="tag">CEU eligible</span> </div> </div> <div class="service-card"> <div class="service-icon">03</div> <div class="service-name">Physical Readiness & Safety</div> <p class="service-desc"> When words stop working, you need a plan. Drawing on jiu-jitsu principles of leverage, control, and disengagement — within the NASW do-no-harm framework — this training covers how to exit a situation safely and professionally. </p> <div class="service-tags"> <span class="tag">Hands-on workshop</span> <span class="tag">No martial arts required</span> <span class="tag">NASW compliant</span> </div> </div> </div> </section>

<hr class="divider">

<!-- GARRISON -->

<section id="garrison"> <div class="garrison-accent-bg"></div> <div class="garrison-layout"> <div class="garrison-left"> <div class="garrison-pill">The Garrison Project</div> <div class="section-label">Mission</div> <h2 class="section-title">The Work<br>That Matters.</h2> <p> GoCo Dynamics is the revenue engine. The Garrison Project is why it exists. </p> <p> The Garrison Project is a nonprofit mentorship program built specifically for fatherless young men. Not another talking circle. Not a feelings journal. <strong>A structured environment where boys learn to build character, earn leadership, and develop real resilience — on the mat and in the classroom.</strong> </p> <p> The program pairs jiu-jitsu training with concrete life skills: getting an ID, navigating college applications, mock interviews, financial basics, and academic support. Success is measured in grades, attendance, and outcomes — not feelings expressed in a session. </p> <a href="#contact" class="btn-primary" style="margin-top: 0.5rem;">Partner with Us</a> </div> <div class="garrison-right"> <div class="outcome-row"> <div class="outcome-icon"></div> <div class="outcome-text"> <strong>Movement-Based Resilience</strong> <span>Jiu-jitsu teaches leverage, control, and working from bad positions — skills that translate directly off the mat.</span> </div> </div> <div class="outcome-row"> <div class="outcome-icon"></div> <div class="outcome-text"> <strong>Academic Accountability</strong> <span>Grade improvement is tracked and celebrated as a concrete outcome. Report cards tell the real story.</span> </div> </div> <div class="outcome-row"> <div class="outcome-icon"></div> <div class="outcome-text"> <strong>Life Skills Curriculum</strong> <span>IDs, applications, interviews, financial basics — the practical tools schools don't teach and fathers often do.</span> </div> </div> <div class="outcome-row"> <div class="outcome-icon"></div> <div class="outcome-text"> <strong>Male-Specific Mentorship</strong> <span>Boys need spaces built for boys, led by men who understand what they're missing. That's the design — by intent.</span> </div> </div> <div class="outcome-row"> <div class="outcome-icon"></div> <div class="outcome-text"> <strong>Outcome-Driven Funding Model</strong> <span>Data-tracked results make the program fundable and defensible. The results talk louder than the politics.</span> </div> </div> </div> </div> </section>

<hr class="divider">

<!-- CONTACT -->

<section id="contact"> <div class="section-label">Contact</div> <h2 class="section-title">Book a<br>Seminar.</h2> <div class="contact-layout"> <div class="contact-left"> <p> Ready to get your team trained? Looking to partner with the Garrison Project? Reach out directly. No gatekeeping, no sales pipeline — just Angelo. </p> <div class="contact-detail"> <div class="contact-detail-item"> <span class="contact-detail-label">Location</span> <span class="contact-detail-value">San Diego, CA &mdash; Available Nationally</span> </div> <div class="contact-detail-item"> <span class="contact-detail-label">Organization</span> <span class="contact-detail-value">GoCo Dynamics LLC</span> </div> <div class="contact-detail-item"> <span class="contact-detail-label">Founder</span> <span class="contact-detail-value">Angelo Coates, MSW Candidate</span> </div> <div class="contact-detail-item"> <span class="contact-detail-label">Email</span> <span class="contact-detail-value"><a href="/cdn-cgi/l/email-protection" class="__cf_email__" data-cfemail="0a6b646d6f666569656b7e6f794a736b62656524696567">[email&#160;protected]</a></span> </div> </div> <p style="font-size: 0.88rem; color: var(--muted); line-height: 1.65;"> GoCo works with social work agencies, nonprofits, school systems, and individual practitioners. Seminar pricing is based on group size and format. Online refresher courses are in development. </p> </div> <div> <form class="contact-form" onsubmit="handleSubmit(event)"> <div class="form-row"> <div class="form-group"> <label for="name">Full Name</label> <input type="text" id="name" placeholder="Your name" required> </div> <div class="form-group"> <label for="org">Organization</label> <input type="text" id="org" placeholder="Agency or school"> </div> </div> <div class="form-group"> <label for="email">Email Address</label> <input type="email" id="email" placeholder="your@email.com" required> </div> <div class="form-group"> <label for="interest">I'm interested in</label> <select id="interest"> <option value="" disabled selected>Select a training</option> <option>Situational Awareness Training</option> <option>Verbal De-Escalation Workshop</option> <option>Physical Readiness & Safety</option> <option>Full-Day Combined Seminar</option> <option>Garrison Project Partnership</option> <option>Other / General Inquiry</option> </select> </div> <div class="form-group"> <label for="message">Message</label> <textarea id="message" placeholder="Tell me about your team, your needs, and what you're looking for."></textarea> </div> <button type="submit" class="form-submit">Send Message</button> <div id="form-confirm" style="display:none; font-family: 'DM Mono', monospace; font-size: 0.75rem; letter-spacing: 0.12em; color: var(--accent); padding-top: 0.5rem;"> Message received. Expect a response within 48 hours. </div> </form> </div> </div> </section>

<!-- FOOTER -->

<footer> <div class="footer-logo">Go<span>Co</span> Dynamics</div> <ul class="footer-links"> <li><a href="#about">About</a></li> <li><a href="#services">Services</a></li> <li><a href="#garrison">Garrison Project</a></li> <li><a href="#contact">Contact</a></li> </ul> <div class="footer-copy">&copy; 2025 GoCo Dynamics LLC &mdash; San Diego, CA</div> </footer>

<script data-cfasync="false" src="/cdn-cgi/scripts/5c5dd728/cloudflare-static/email-decode.min.js"></script><script>

function handleSubmit(e) { e.preventDefault(); const name = document.getElementById(‘name’).value; const org = document.getElementById(‘org’).value; const email = document.getElementById(‘email’).value; const interest = document.getElementById(‘interest’).value; const message = document.getElementById(‘message’).value;

const subject = encodeURIComponent('GoCo Dynamics Inquiry: ' + (interest || 'General'));
const body = encodeURIComponent(
  'Name: ' + name +
  '\nOrganization: ' + org +
  '\nEmail: ' + email +
  '\nInterest: ' + interest +
  '\n\nMessage:\n' + message
);

window.location.href = 'mailto:angelocoates@yahoo.com?subject=' + subject + '&body=' + body;
document.getElementById('form-confirm').style.display = 'block';
e.target.reset();

}

// Subtle nav shrink on scroll window.addEventListene

Mar 19

Claude responded: Found the problem.

Found the problem. GitHub is scrambling the CSS variable dashes. Look at your code — everywhere it should say var(--black) it got changed to var(–black) with a single dash. That's why the colors and layout are broken. The stat strip is also still in the CSS even though it's gone from the HTML.

Don't paste from GitHub — that version is corrupted. Use the clean file I have here instead.

Presented file

Download that file directly. Then go to GitHub, open index.html, tap the pencil, select ALL the text, delete it, then paste from the downloaded file — not from anything you copied out of GitHub. The GitHub editor is mangling the dashes when you copy back out of it.

The downloaded file has the correct code with proper -- double dashes throughout. That's the one that works.

Goco dynamics
Code · HTML 

You said: Where do I find where I can click on the website to go to it from railway

Where do I find where I can click on the website to go to it from railway

Mar 19

Claude responded: Tap Settings at the top of that screen, then scroll down to Networking.

Tap Settings at the top of that screen, then scroll down to Networking. Your URL will be listed there.

Or just type it directly in your browser: goco-dynamics-production.up.railway.app

You said: It’s the same

It’s the same

Mar 19

Claude responded: Still serving the old cached version.

Still serving the old cached version. The stat strip HTML is gone from the file but the browser is holding onto the old one.

The real issue is doing this from your phone. GitHub mobile is corrupting the dashes every time you paste. We need a different approach.

Two options:

Option 1 — Delete the repo and start clean from a computer. If you have access to a laptop or desktop at any point, this takes 5 minutes and we do it right.

Option 2 — Use GitHub's web editor on your phone but type the fix manually. Instead of pasting the whole file, just delete the stat strip lines. Go to GitHub, open index.html, tap the pencil, then use Find (if your browser supports it) to search for hero-stat-strip and delete just those lines.

Specifically find and delete this block — it starts with:

<div class="hero-stat-strip">

And ends about 15 lines later with the closing:

</div>

That's the only thing causing the overlap. Everything else on the site is working.

Do you have access to a computer at any point, or are we doing this phone-only?

You said: Option 1

Option 1

Mar 19

Claude responded: Perfect.

Perfect. When you get to a computer, here's exactly what to do. Takes 5 minutes.

Step 1 — Delete the current repo

Go to github.com, open the Goco-Dynamics repo, click Settings, scroll all the way to the bottom to the Danger Zone, click Delete this repository, confirm it.

Step 2 — Create a fresh one

Click + → New repository, name it goco-dynamics, set to Public, check Add a README, click Create repository.

Step 3 — Create index.html

Click Add file → Create new file, name it index.html, then copy and paste the code from the file below.

Step 4 — Add package.json

Same thing — Add file → Create new file, name it package.json, paste this:

json
{
  "name": "goco-dynamics",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "npx serve . -l 8080"
  }
}

Step 5 — Reconnect Railway

Go to Railway, delete the current Goco-Dynamics project, create a new one, connect it to the new GitHub repo, generate the domain on port 8080. Done.

Here's the clean file to copy from:

Presented file

Save that file. When you sit down at a computer, come back to this conversation and we'll knock it out clean.

Goco dynamics
Code · HTML 

You said: Let’s just make the changes from mobile.

Let’s just make the changes from mobile. It looks good but the background on my laptop using the old link to the website. Thoughts?

Mar 19

Claude responded: That looks solid on desktop.

That looks solid on desktop. The layout is clean, nav is working, stat strip is sitting right where it should on the right side. That's the designed version.

The background should be dark — it's showing light/white right now which means the CSS variables are still broken from the phone edits. The --black background isn't rendering.

You're on the laptop right now. Let's fix it properly in 2 minutes.

Go to your GitHub repo, open index.html, click the pencil to edit. Then hit Ctrl+A to select all the code and delete it. Then open the file I'm sharing below, hit Ctrl+A to select all, Ctrl+C to copy, go back to GitHub and Ctrl+V to paste. Then commit.

Presented file

That file has the correct code with the dark background and no stat strip overlap on mobile. Paste that in and commit — Railway redeploys automatically and it'll look right on both desktop and phone.

Claude responded: Still serving the corrupted code — white background, blue text.

Still serving the corrupted code — white background, blue text. The paste didn't take. The CSS variables are still broken.

Here's what to do right now on your laptop:

  1. Go to your GitHub repo
  2. Click on index.html
  3. Click the pencil icon to edit
  4. Press Ctrl+A to select everything
  5. Press Delete
  6. Now open the downloaded goco-dynamics.html file in a text editor — right click it → Open with → Notepad
  7. Press Ctrl+A then Ctrl+C to copy everything
  8. Go back to GitHub and press Ctrl+V to paste
  9. Scroll down and click Commit changes

The key is opening the file in Notepad first, not copying from the browser or Claude's preview. Notepad will give you the raw clean code without any formatting corruption.

Claude responded: The code looks perfect this time — no corruption, clean hex colors, everything right.

The code looks perfect this time — no corruption, clean hex colors, everything right. The problem is your browser is caching the old version. The site is actually fixed, your browser just doesn't know it yet.

Do this right now:

Hold Ctrl and tap F5 on your keyboard at the same time. That forces a hard refresh and clears the cache.

Claude responded: Ok.

Ok. The code is clean — the issue is GitHub still has the old broken version. Let me confirm.

Go to your GitHub repo right now and click on index.html. Don't edit it — just look at the first few lines. Does it say:

body { background: #0a0a0a;

background: var(–black);

Mar 19

Claude responded: Two problems right there in that screenshot.

Two problems right there in that screenshot.

First — you have both Index.html and index.html in the repo. Two separate files. Railway is serving Index.html with the capital I, which is the old broken one. The new clean code is in index.html lowercase but Railway isn't reading that one.

Second — the new file still has CSS variables (--black, --off-black) instead of the plain hex codes, which means the paste didn't use the latest version.

Fix is simple. Delete Index.html — the one with the capital I. That's the broken one Railway is using.

Click on Index.html in the file list, click the three dots ... on the right side, click Delete file, commit the deletion.

Once that's gone Railway will automatically use index.html and the site should render correctly. Do that and check the site again.

That's the original broken file — still has var(–black) with single dashes throughout. The index.html lowercase file in your repo never got updated with the clean code.

Here's the simplest fix. You're on your laptop right now. Do this:

  1. Go to your GitHub repo
  2. Click on index.html (lowercase)
  3. Click the pencil icon to edit
  4. Press Ctrl+A to select everything and delete it
  5. Copy everything from the code block below — click at the very start, scroll to the bottom, Shift+click at the end to select it all, then Ctrl+C
  6. Paste into GitHub with Ctrl+V
  7. Scroll down, click Commit changes

The key thing — copy from HERE, not from Notepad, not from anywhere else. The clean version is right below.

Claude responded: Got it.

Got it. The nav is covering the top of the hero. Easy fix — the hero needs more top padding to push the content down below the nav bar.

Fix hero top padding so content isn't hidden behind fixed nav

Go to GitHub, open index.html, pencil to edit, find this line:

padding: 0 4rem 6rem;

Change just that part to:

padding: 5rem 4rem 6rem;

Commit. That's a one word change — no need to replace the whole file. Railway redeploys and the headline will sit fully visible below the nav.

Claude responded: Press Ctrl+F in the GitHub editor to open find, then search for:

padding: 0 4rem 6rem;

It'll jump right to it. Change the 0 to 5rem and commit.

Same structure as GoCo, different brand. Let me build it.

Ran a command, read a file

There it is. Navy #1B2A4A base, gold #C9961A accent — straight off the rash guard. Four pages:

Home — Built on the Mat. Proven in Life. Free program, ages 12-17, BJJ methodology in the stat strip.

About — Five credentials. Challenge over comfort framing. Founded by Angelo and Eder.

Program — Three cards: Intake and Relationship, Jiu Jitsu Mentorship, Life Skills Sessions.

Values — Proactive over Reactive, Challenge over Comfort, Results over Rhetoric — pulled directly from the binder's core values.

Contact — Get Involved. Referrals, volunteers, partners, funders. All roads to the same form.

To deploy: create a new GitHub repo called garrison-project, add index.html with this code and the same package.json as GoCo, connect to Railway, generate domain on port 8080. Same exact process.

Double-click the file locally first to confirm it looks right before you push it.

Garrison project
Code · HTML 

You said: give me the steps again

give me the steps again

Mar 19

Claude responded: Same process as GoCo.

Same process as GoCo. Here it is clean:

Step 1 — Create a new GitHub repo

  • Go to github.com
  • Click + → New repository
  • Name it garrison-project
  • Set to Public
  • Check Add a README file
  • Click Create repository

Step 2 — Add index.html

  • Click Add file → Create new file
  • Name it index.html
  • Open the garrison-project.html file in Notepad, Ctrl+A, Ctrl+C
  • Paste into GitHub
  • Click Commit changes

Step 3 — Add package.json

  • Click Add file → Create new file
  • Name it package.json
  • Paste this:
json
{
  "name": "garrison-project",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "npx serve . -l 8080"
  }
}
  • Click Commit changes

Step 4 — Deploy on Railway

  • Go to railway.com
  • Click New Project
  • Click Deploy from GitHub repo
  • Select garrison-project
  • Wait for ACTIVE and green checkmarks

Step 5 — Get your URL

  • Click Settings → Networking → Generate Domain
  • Enter 8080 as the port
  • Copy your live URL

That's it.

You said: OK, it worked perfectly so now what is the next step to make this live and have it forward to like my personal email and what would I have to do when I get it?

OK, it worked perfectly so now what is the next step to make this live and have it forward to like my personal email and what would I have to do when I get it? Should I get two emails one for Garrison referrals and contacts and Goco or can I have them both funnel to the same place?

Mar 19

Claude responded: Good.

Good. Here is the full answer.

On the email question — one or two?

Get two separate email addresses. Not because you can't funnel them to the same inbox — you can — but because the people contacting each one are different and the first impression matters.

  • info@gocodynamics.com — agencies, social work orgs, funders booking seminars
  • info@garrisonproject.org — parents referring kids, schools, volunteers, donors

Both forward to your Yahoo. You see everything in one place. But whoever contacts you sees a professional address that matches the organization they're reaching out to. That's the credibility piece.

Steps to make it all live:

1 — Buy your domains

Go to Namecheap.com or GoDaddy. Buy both:

  • gocodynamics.com
  • garrisonproject.org (nonprofits use .org)

Should be about $10-15 each per year.

2 — Connect domains to Railway

For each site, go to Railway → Settings → Networking → Custom Domain. Type in the domain. Railway gives you DNS records to copy. You paste those into Namecheap or GoDaddy's DNS settings. Takes about 10-30 minutes to go live after that.

3 — Set up professional email

Cheapest option is Google Workspace — $6/month per email address. Gives you Gmail but with your custom domain. You set up forwarding so everything hits your Yahoo or you just work out of the Workspace inbox directly.

Alternatively Zoho Mail has a free tier for custom domain email if you want to start at zero cost.

4 — Update the contact forms

Once you have the professional emails set up, come back here and I update both sites to send form submissions to the new addresses instead of the Yahoo.

Order of operations:

Buy domains first. Connect to Railway second. Email third. That's the sequence. What do you want to tackle first?

You said: Garrisonproject.

Garrisonproject.org and the garrison project.org are taken

Mar 19

Claude responded: Common.

Common. Here are your options:

Closest alternatives to check:

  • thegarrisonproject.com
  • garrisonprojectsd.org
  • garrisonprojectbjj.org
  • garrison-project.org
  • garrisonprojectyouth.org

For GoCo — check these too:

  • gocodynamics.com
  • gocodyn.com
  • goco-dynamics.com

Before you buy anything — go to Namecheap.com and search each one. It'll show you instantly what's available and what it costs. Stick to .com for GoCo and .org for Garrison since it's a nonprofit.

One thing worth knowing — if garrisonproject.org is taken but nobody is actually using it as an active site, you can sometimes contact the owner through a WHOIS lookup and buy it directly. But that gets complicated and expensive fast. Easier to just pick a clean alternative.

What does Namecheap show as available?

Claude responded: Go with a different name for the Garrison site then.

Go with a different name for the Garrison site then. You have options:

Built around the mission:

  • buildingresiliencesd.org
  • mattolife.org
  • earnednotgiven.org
  • provenonthemat.org

Built around the population:

  • fatherlesstoformidable.org
  • raisedright-sd.org
  • boystomentors.org

Built around the brand you already have:

  • garrisonbjj.org
  • garrisonsdyouth.org
  • garrisonmentor.org

My honest recommendation — earnednotgiven.org. It's direct, it's on brand, it says everything about the program philosophy without explaining it. Uniforms are earned. A spot is earned. That's the whole thing in three words.

Second choice — provenonthemat.org. Ties directly to the tagline already on the site.

Check those on Namecheap and tell me what's available. We pick one, update the site copy to reflect it, and move forward.

Here are more — different angles:

Stoic / Character-based:

My top pick out of all of these — builtnotborn.org. Short, hard, says exactly what the program believes. Nobody is born resilient. You build it. That's the whole philosophy in three words.

Second — standingpost.org. A post stands whether anyone notices or not. That's the stoic angle right there.

What lands for you?

I’m using the same steps to build a website for my sister-in-law, but I need to know the prompt to send to her specific chat. That’s in a different project.

Mar 19

Claude responded: Got it.

Got it. Here's a prompt she can paste into a new Claude conversation to get started:

"I need help building a website and deploying it on GitHub and Railway. I want a professional single-page website for [describe her business — what it is, who it serves, what she offers]. The site needs these sections: [list the pages she wants — e.g. Home, About, Services, Contact]. The primary goal of the site is [establish credibility / book clients / sell products]. Her main audience is [who will land on the site]. Her brand colors are [any colors she has or pre


Claude's response was interrupted.

You said: No, I just need the steps when we get to get hub and we start creating new files and then adding like all the code and stuff to it

No, I just need the steps when we get to get hub and we start creating new files and then adding like all the code and stuff to it

Mar 19

Claude responded: Here are the steps:

Here are the steps:

1 — Create the repo

  • Go to github.com
  • Click + → New repository
  • Name it whatever the site is called
  • Set to Public
  • Check Add a README file
  • Click Create repository

2 — Add index.html

  • Click Add file → Create new file
  • Name it exactly index.html (all lowercase)
  • Paste all the website code in
  • Click Commit changes

3 — Add package.json

  • Click Add file → Create new file
  • Name it exactly package.json (all lowercase)
  • Paste this in:
json
{
  "name": "your-site-name",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "npx serve . -l 8080"
  }
}
  • Click Commit changes

4 — Deploy on Railway

  • Go to railway.com
  • Click New Project → Deploy from GitHub repo
  • Select the repo
  • Wait for ACTIVE and green checkmarks
  • Go to Settings → Networking → Generate Domain
  • Enter 8080 as the port
  • Copy the live URL

That's the whole process. Send that to her and she's good to go.

You said: How do i get my website tab to have an icon like the ones in the photo using git hub and railway if possible

How do i get my website tab to have an icon like the ones in the photo using git hub and railway if possible

Mar 22

Claude responded: That's called a favicon — the small icon that shows in the browser tab.

That's called a favicon — the small icon that shows in the browser tab.

Easiest way to do it:

Step 1 — Create your favicon

Go to favicon.io — it's free. You can:

  • Type text (like a "G" for Garrison or "GC" for GoCo) and pick colors to match your brand
  • It generates the icon files automatically

Download the zip file it gives you.

Step 2 — Upload to GitHub

Inside the zip you'll find a file called favicon.ico. Go to your GitHub repo:

  • Click Add file → Upload files
  • Upload just the favicon.ico file
  • Commit it

Step 3 — Add one line to your HTML

Go to your index.html, click the pencil to edit, and add this line inside the <head> section right below the <title> line:

html
<link rel="icon" type="image/x-icon" href="/favicon.ico">

Commit. Railway redeploys automatically and your tab will show the icon.

For GoCo — use a dark background with orange "GC" or just "G". For Garrison — navy background with gold "G". Matches the brand exactly.

Want me to write out exactly where in the code to paste that line?

You said: WHAT FONT?

WHAT FONT?

Mar 22

Claude responded: From your binder — the Garrison Project logo uses a bold condensed serif.

From your binder — the Garrison Project logo uses a bold condensed serif. Closest match that's free on Google Fonts is Oswald or Barlow Condensed.

But honestly for the favicon it doesn't matter much — it's tiny. At that size a clean bold letter in the right colors is what reads. The font becomes secondary.

For favicon.io just pick Bold weight and your colors:

  • Garrison — navy background #1B2A4A, gold text #C9961A, letter G
  • GoCo — dark background #0a0a0a, orange text #c8410a, letters GC

That will look sharp in the tab. What font is on the actual Garrison logo in your binder — do you know what it is?

You said: whats the font from the website

whats the font from the website

Mar 22

Claude responded: The websites use Bebas Neue for all the big headings and DM Mono for the small label text.

The websites use Bebas Neue for all the big headings and DM Mono for the small label text.

For the favicon I'd go Bebas Neue — it's already on your site, it's bold and clean, and a single letter in that font at small size will look sharp.

On favicon.io just type the font name in the font field and it should pull it up.

You said: its telling me to: First, use the download button to download the files listed below.

its telling me to: First, use the download button to download the files listed below. Place the files in the root directory of your website. how do i do that?

Mar 22

Claude responded: I can see it already worked — look at your browser tab in the screenshot.

I can see it already worked — look at your browser tab in the screenshot. It says GC in the tab right now. You already have a favicon showing.

But if you want to do it properly with all those files:

Step 1 — Download the zip from favicon.io

Click the Download button on that page. It saves a zip file to your computer.

Step 2 — Extract the zip

Right click the zip file → Extract All. Opens a folder with all those files listed.

Step 3 — Upload all files to GitHub

Go to your GitHub repo → Add file → Upload files. Drag all the extracted files in at once. Commit.

Step 4 — Add the link tags to your HTML

Go back to favicon.io, scroll down to that code block and click Copy. Then go to GitHub, open index.html, click the pencil, find the <head> section near the top, and paste those lines right after the <title> line. Commit.

That's it. Railway redeploys and every device — phone, tablet, desktop — gets the right icon size automatically.

But again — your tab already shows GC so the basic version is already working.

Mar 22

Claude responded: Scroll back up to the top of the favicon.

Scroll back up to the top of the favicon.io page. The Download button is at the top of the page right above the Installation instructions you're looking at now.

You said: walk me through all of step 3 again

walk me through all of step 3 again

Mar 22

Claude responded: Step 3 — Upload all files to GitHub

Step 3 — Upload all files to GitHub

  1. Go to github.com and open your Goco-Dynamics repo
  2. Click Add file → Upload files
  3. Open File Explorer on your computer and find the favicon folder you extracted
  4. Select all the files inside it — press Ctrl+A to select all
  5. Drag them all into the GitHub upload box
  6. Scroll down and click Commit changes

Then do the exact same thing for your garrison-project repo.

You said: goco worked just fine but the garrison favicon isnt working

goco worked just fine but the garrison favicon isnt working

Mar 22

Claude responded: The favicon code probably isn't in the Garrison site's HTML yet.

The favicon code probably isn't in the Garrison site's HTML yet.

Go to your garrison-project GitHub repo, open index.html, click the pencil to edit, and find this line near the top:

html
<title>The Garrison Project | Building Resilience Through Jiu Jitsu</title>

Right below that line paste the favicon link tags from favicon.io. Should look something like this:

html
<link rel="apple-touch-icon" sizes="180x180" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="32x32" href="/favicon-32x32.png">
<link rel="icon" type="image/png" sizes="16x16" href="/favicon-16x16.png">
<link rel="manifest" href="/site.webmanifest">

Commit. Railway redeploys and the favicon shows up.

Make sure you also uploaded the favicon files to the garrison-project repo — not just the GoCo one. Each repo needs its own copy of the files.

You said: now im getting this oage after railway deployed it

now im getting this oage after railway deployed it

Mar 22

Claude responded: The site.

The site.webmanifest file that came with the favicon package is crashing the server. Easy fix.

Go to your garrison-project GitHub repo and check if there's a site.webmanifest file in there. If there is — open it, click the three dots ..., and delete it. Commit.

Railway will redeploy automatically and the site will come back up. The favicon will still work without that file.

You said: How can using fable 5 make these websites better?

How can using fable 5 make these websites better?

9:18 PM

Claude responded: That's me — you're talking to Fable 5 right now in this conversation.

Identified self-reference and pivoted toward practical application guidance

That's me — you're talking to Fable 5 right now in this conversation. The model picker in that screenshot is for whatever chat you had open there; this project chat is where the website work lives.

Here's what I can actually do to level these sites up:

Stronger design work. The current sites are solid single-page builds. I can take them further — multi-page structures, better visual hierarchy, custom graphics in SVG (shield marks, section dividers that match the rash guard styling), scroll animations that feel intentional instead of templated.

Smarter problem solving. The debugging we did — the dash corruption, the port issues, the webmanifest crash — I catch that stuff faster and give you the right fix the first time more often.

Real functionality. Contact forms that actually email you without opening the visitor's mail app (using a free service like Formspree). A referral intake form for Garrison that collects parent info properly. A seminar booking flow for GoCo.

Content depth. Testimonial sections ready for when you have them. A data/outcomes page for Garrison built to receive your grade-tracking results — the thing funders will actually look at. CEU documentation pages for GoCo.

The honest answer though — the model matters less than what you ask for. The sites work. The next real gains are functionality (working forms) and content (outcomes data when you have it).

What's the next thing you want on either site? The Formspree contact form upgrade is probably the highest-value move — right now your form opens the visitor's email app, which loses people.

You said: What would you recommended?

What would you recommended? I’m a couple months away from having the money to pay for the websites. But I would like to have these websites up and running in perfect so that when I can buy the websites which I might actually do in the next week and a half when I get paid.

9:26 PM

Claude responded: Here's how I'd sequence it.

Prioritized free optimization improvements before domain purchase

Here's how I'd sequence it. Everything on this list is free — so when you buy the domains in a week and a half, you're connecting them to finished sites, not works in progress.

Priority 1 — Working contact forms (do this first)

Right now your forms open the visitor's email app. On a phone, that fails half the time and you lose the lead. Formspree is free for 50 submissions a month — the form submits directly on the site and the message lands in your inbox. For a parent referring their kid or an agency asking about a seminar, this is the difference between getting the contact and not. Takes about 15 minutes per site to set up.

Priority 2 — Mobile pass on both sites

Most parents and most agency staff will open these on a phone. We fixed the worst of it on GoCo, but both sites deserve one clean review — spacing, font sizes, button sizes on small screens. Send me screenshots of each site on your phone and I'll fix anything that's off.

Priority 3 — A Garrison referral section

GoCo's "book a seminar" flow makes sense already. Garrison's weakest point is that a parent landing there doesn't see a clear "here's how my son joins" path. A short section — who qualifies, what it costs (nothing), what happens after you reach out — turns visitors into referrals.

Priority 4 — Small credibility details

Favicon is done. Worth adding: a real meta description for each site (what shows up in Google results), and an Open Graph preview so when you text someone the link it shows a clean card instead of a bare URL.

When you get paid:

Buy both domains (~$25 total/year), connect to Railway, done — sites are already perfect at that point. Hold off on Google Workspace email until there's real contact volume; Zoho's free tier covers custom-domain email at zero cost until then.

Want to start with the Formspree forms? That's the highest-value 15 minutes on this list.

You said: Here’s the websites.

Here’s the websites. I want to make sure the language in more inclusive not just fatherless boys: https://garrison-project-production.up.railway.app/

9:34 PM

Claude responded: Quick check before I make edits — "inclusive" can mean two different things here, and they're very different calls:

Reconciled inclusive language with founder's male-specific program design

Quick check before I make edits — "inclusive" can mean two different things here, and they're very different calls:

  1. Broader than "fatherless" — covering boys whose dads are absent, incarcerated, deployed, working three jobs, or just not present. Still boys-only, but the doorway is wider than the literal word "fatherless."
  2. Gender-inclusive — opening the language to girls, which changes the program's core design.

Based on everything you've built, I'm assuming you mean the first one — the binder and program design are explicitly male-specific and that's been the point from day one. "Fatherless" as a word is also doing you no favors with referral sources: a school counselor won't refer a kid whose dad is technically around but checked out, because the site says "fatherless" and that kid doesn't qualify on paper.

If that's right, here are the two edits. Both are quick find-and-replace in GitHub — no full repaste needed.

Edit 1 — Hero section. Find:

A free mentorship program for fatherless boys ages 12-17.

Replace with:

A free mentorship program for boys ages 12-17 growing up without a consistent father figure.

Edit 2 — Life Skills card. Find:

The practical tools schools skip and fathers often provide.

Replace with:

The practical tools schools skip and nobody else is teaching.

That covers every kid you actually want — divorced homes, incarcerated dads, deployed dads, absent dads — without diluting who the program is for. The "Male-Specific Design" credential stays as is, since that's the design, not the gatekeeping.

If you meant option 2 — opening it to girls — that's a bigger conversation than website copy, because the whole program framework in your binder is built around the male-specific model. Tell me which one you meant and I'll adjust.

You said: I like the male specific design.

I like the male specific design. Let’s keep it like that. No girls for now. What do we need to do to get this updated on railway and git hub? I just activated my hobby account for railway. We can fix the garrison site and then the goco one after. Let implement all the changes that we can do with fable.

9:38 PM

Claude responded: Good.

Architected complete site rebuild with Formspree integration and deployment strategy

Good. Locking in male-specific. Here's everything I'm baking into the Garrison site in one pass: the two language edits, a "How to Join" referral section for parents, meta description and link-preview tags, the favicon links, and a real contact form through Formspree. Then I'll give you the GitHub steps.

Edited 5 files, ran 3 commands, read a file

All changes are in and verified clean — zero "fatherless" left, no special characters that could corrupt in the paste. Here's what's new:

  1. Hero — "boys ages 12-17 growing up without a consistent father figure"
  2. Life Skills card — "the practical tools schools skip and nobody else is teaching"
  3. New "How to Join" section — three steps: Reach Out, Meet Us, Start Training. Student-first intake, completely free, parent and counselor friendly
  4. Meta description + link preview tags — Google results and texted links now show a clean description
  5. Favicon links — in the head, pointing to the files you already uploaded
  6. Formspree form — submits directly on the site, no more email-app popup

One thing you have to do before the form works — get your Formspree ID:

  1. Go to formspree.io → Sign up free with your Yahoo
  2. Click New Form, name it "Garrison Project"
  3. It gives you a form endpoint that looks like https://formspree.io/f/abcd1234
  4. Copy that 8-character code at the end

Then update GitHub:

  1. Download the file above, open in Notepad, Ctrl+A, Ctrl+C
  2. GitHub → garrison-project repo → index.html → pencil → Ctrl+A, delete, paste → Commit
  3. Then one more edit: Ctrl+F for YOUR_FORM_ID and replace it with your real Formspree code → Commit

Railway auto-deploys. Form submissions land straight in your inbox.

Get the Formspree ID set up and tell me when Garrison is live — then we run the same play on GoCo.

Garrison project
Code · HTML

when i go to download it it just takes me to the website

9:52 PM

Claude responded: Right click the download link → Save link as → save it to your Downloads folder.

Right click the download link → Save link as → save it to your Downloads folder. That forces the save instead of opening it in the browser.

Fable is the most capable model and draws down usage 2× faster than Opus
